serving.natural_language_portion: One standard plate of Chicken Aglio Olio as served in a Singaporean restaurant typically weighs approximately 400 g and constitutes a complete single-person meal.

nutrient_source.title: USDA FoodData Central — Pasta with meat sauce (SR Legacy) and Chicken, broilers or fryers, meat only, cooked, roasted (SR Legacy); composite estimation for Chicken Aglio Olio
nutrient_source.organization: U.S. Department of Agriculture, Agricultural Research Service
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No exact entry for Chicken Aglio Olio exists in USDA FoodData Central or Singapore HPB Foodo database. Nutrient values were estimated by compositing: (1) USDA SR Legacy — spaghetti cooked (FDC ID 20124), (2) USDA SR Legacy — chicken breast meat cooked roasted (FDC ID 05064), (3) USDA SR Legacy — olive oil (FDC ID 04053), (4) USDA SR Legacy — garlic raw (FDC ID 11215). Input record values (provenance: ai_generated, confidence 0.7) were cross-checked against this composite and retained where consistent. Per-100g values reflect an estimated dish composition of approximately 50% spaghetti, 30% chicken, 15% olive oil and garlic, 5% seasoning. Values are consistent with the input record and are plausible for this dish type.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008 — Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC; spaghetti white boiled (GI ~46–58, mean ~52) used as analogous base; adjusted for chicken and olive oil dilution effect
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / American Journal of Clinical Nutrition
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Chicken Aglio Olio. GI estimated using white spaghetti (boiled) as the primary carbohydrate vehicle, GI ~52 (mean of published range 46–58) per University of Sydney GI Database and Atkinson et al. 2008 (Am J Clin Nutr 88:1498–1517). Olive oil and protein from chicken reduce postprandial glycemic response; composite dish GI estimated at 52 (conservative, unadjusted for fat-protein dampening). Glycemic load per serving calculated as: GL = (GI × available carbohydrate per serving) / 100 = (52 × 60.0) / 100 = 31.2, where available carbohydrate per 400g serving = 15.0 g/100g × 4 = 60.0 g.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium 300 mg/100g; a 400g serving delivers approximately 1200 mg sodium, representing 52–60% of the WHO daily limit of 2000 mg
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should request reduced-salt preparation and limit portion size to 200–250g to keep sodium intake below 600–750 mg per meal; olive oil and garlic are DASH-diet-aligned and support endothelial function, partially offsetting sodium risk.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Refined spaghetti base contributes 15g carbohydrate/100g with only 1.0g dietary fibre, yielding a glycemic load of approximately 31 per standard 400g serving
type2_diabetes.guidance: People with Type 2 Diabetes should limit portion size to 150–200g, substitute whole-grain or legume-based pasta to increase fibre and lower GI, and increase the chicken-to-pasta ratio to improve the protein-to-carbohydrate balance and blunt postprandial glucose excursion.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Saturated fat is low at 1.75g/100g; olive oil provides predominantly monounsaturated fat (~5.2g/100g estimated) with cardioprotective oleic acid; trans fat is negligible at 0.05g/100g
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Chicken Aglio Olio is broadly suitable for individuals managing cardiovascular disease due to its low saturated fat content and olive oil base; sodium at 300mg/100g warrants portion control, and requesting extra-virgin olive oil maximises cardioprotective polyphenol content.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Chicken Aglio Olio is an Italian-inspired pasta dish adapted for the Singaporean palate, consisting of spaghetti tossed in extra-virgin olive oil with generous amounts of thinly sliced or minced garlic, dried chili flakes, and tender pieces of pan-seared or sautéed chicken breast or thigh. The dish is finished with black pepper, salt, and fresh or dried parsley. In Singapore, it is commonly served at Western-style hawker stalls, casual dining restaurants, and Indian-run Western food stalls, where it may carry additional spice from chili padi or curry-adjacent seasoning. The texture is silky and lightly oiled, with al dente spaghetti strands coated in fragrant garlic-infused oil. The flavour profile is savoury, garlicky, mildly spicy, and umami-forward from the chicken. Nutritionally, a 400g plate provides approximately 750 kcal, 45g protein, 35g fat, and 60g carbohydrate. Saturated fat is low at 1.75g/100g, making it more cardiovascular-friendly than cream-based pasta dishes. Sodium at 300mg/100g is moderate and warrants attention for hypertensive individuals. The glycemic load per serving is approximately 31, placing it in the moderate-to-high range for blood glucose management. Olive oil contributes oleocanthal, a natural anti-inflammatory compound, and monounsaturated fatty acids beneficial for lipid profiles. Garlic provides allicin with evidence-based cardiovascular and anti-inflammatory benefits. This dish is suitable for halal diets and is dairy-free and egg-free in its standard preparation.

confidence_reason: No exact entry for Chicken Aglio Olio exists in USDA FoodData Central or Singapore HPB Foodo database. Nutrient values were estimated by compositing USDA SR Legacy entries for cooked spaghetti, roasted chicken breast, olive oil, and garlic, and cross-checked against the input record (ai_generated, confidence 0.7). The composite estimation is internally consistent and plausible, but has not been validated against a laboratory analysis of this specific dish. Confidence is therefore medium.

Individual component values could be estimated but are not reported here to avoid compounding estimation error. 3. potassium_mg (210.0 mg/100g) and monounsaturated_fat_g (5.2 g/100g) and polyunsaturated_fat_g (1.1 g/100g) are estimated from USDA SR Legacy component proportions. 4. GI of 52 is an analogous estimate based on white spaghetti (boiled) per University of Sydney GI Database; no direct GI measurement exists for Chicken Aglio Olio. 5. The input record lists primary_cuisine as 'Indian', reflecting the restaurant context (Indian-run Western stall); the canonical cuisine classification has been updated to 'Italian-Singaporean Fusion' to reflect the dish's actual culinary identity. 6. Halal status is assumed based on standard preparation with chicken and olive oil; individual restaurant certification should be verified. 7.
